Donate

Our History

A legacy conceived in 1942, born from a desire to aid captured men following the Battle of Bataan.

The forerunner of The American Ex-Prisoners of War organization (AXPOW) was first conceived in 1942, by two mothers from New Mexico whose sons had been taken prisoner by the Japanese in the early part of America’s involvement in World War II. Their purpose was to send aid to the captured men taken as POWs following the Battle of Bataan in the Philippines.


That purpose evolved in scope into a national organization geared to help individuals, both military and non-military, captured during various conflicts where circumstances unfolded to render them prisoners of war. AXPOW eventually grew into a Congressionally chartered non-profit veterans' service organization.


Chief Executive Statement

"Preserving the history of American POWs and educating the public about the sacrifices they made and their contribution to America’s wartime fortunes only grows in importance, lest future generations lose personal contact with the realities the wartime experience once so commonly felt."

If taking care of our POWs directly has diminished, the work of reinforcing the stirring memory and the lasting value of their service goes on. This work advances in myriad ways including two recent initiatives that our Chief Executive Officer details.

AXPOW
